caps.proposition_text: Ms. Ramirez has a past medical history significant for hypertension.

evidence.span_text: Ms. Ramirez is a 34-year-old female with a past medical history significant for hypertension, who presents today with back pain.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician encouraged the patient to continue with home monitoring.

evidence.span_text: • Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged her to continue with home monitoring and report any elevated blood pressures to me.

caps.proposition_text: The patient reports weightlifting around 3 times a week.

evidence.span_text: The patient reports she has been walking a lot lately, 30 minutes to an hour or so a day. While walking 6 days ago, she felt her back seize up on her. She is not sure what caused it but thinks she was walking a little faster than usual. She describes the sensation as a spasm. She has been taking ibuprofen and using heat on it but it is still pretty stiff. She denies any known trauma or injury to her back. She notes she has been weightlifting around 3 times a week. She did do deadlifts that day before her walk and thinks she may have been using her back more than her legs to lift. She locates the pain in her mid lower back. She denies any lower extremity numbness, tingling, and weakness.

caps.proposition_text: The patient describes the sensation as a spasm.

evidence.span_text: • Musculoskeletal: Pain to palpation along the right lateral aspect of her lumbar spine. Pain with flexion and extension. Negative straight leg raise.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician assesses low back pain.

evidence.span_text: • Musculoskeletal: Endorses bilateral low back pain and stiffness.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician suspects a lumbar strain.

evidence.span_text: • Medical Reasoning: She experienced a spasm-like pain in her back while walking approximately 6 days ago. She has also been lifting weights recently. Her lumbar spine x-ray was unremarkable and her recent labs were normal. I believe she has a lumbar strain.
